core_story.problem: While initially explored in classification mod- els [4, 13, 14, 36], MU in generative models, particularly diffusion-based ones, poses unique challenges due to the models’ complex, distribution-based learning.

core_story.summary: While initially explored in classification mod- els [4, 13, 14, 36], MU in generative models, particularly diffusion-based ones, poses unique challenges due to the models’ complex, distribution-based learning. However, this widespread use raises critical concerns about data privacy, security, fairness, and intellectual property, as these models rely on vast datasets that often include sensitive, personal, or copyrighted content [35]. Further, the paper offers preliminary insights into the plausible explanation for the instability of the mapping- based unlearning methods that can guide future research toward more robust unlearning techniques. We introduce a framework that includes a couple of measures for analyzing the stability of existing unlearning methods. In this paper, for the first time, through an extensive study, we demonstrate the unstable nature of existing unlearning methods in text-to-image diffusion mod- els.

1. Introduction
The past decade has witnessed a rapid qualitative growth
in text-to-image models [7, 23, 24, 32, 33], leading to
its widespread attention and adoption. Models such as
DALL·E [2, 30, 31], MidJourney [22], and Stable Dif-
fusion [27, 32] enable high-quality image generation from
simple text prompts, opening applications across art, de-
sign, advertising, gaming, and even medical imaging. With
millions worldwide adopting these tools for academic and
commercial purposes, their transformative potential is ev-
ident, as illustrated by MidJourney’s 16 million active

by DALL·E and Stable Diffusion [1, 37]. However, this
widespread use raises critical concerns about data privacy,
security, fairness, and intellectual property, as these models
rely on vast datasets that often include sensitive, personal,
or copyrighted content [35].

introduction_structure.evidence_text:
art methods for text-to-image models. Specifically, we
observe that when these unlearned models undergo fine-
tuning, even with general or unrelated concepts, the pre-
viously unlearned concepts can resurface in the generated
images. This unintended revival undermines the unlearning
process’s effectiveness and poses significant risks to data
privacy and legal compliance. The ability of an unlearned
model to regenerate sensitive or copyrighted content after
benign fine-tuning highlights a fundamental weakness in
the existing MU approaches for generative models. Such
vulnerabilities could lead to unauthorized dissemination of
protected content, violating individual privacy rights and in-
tellectual property laws. Therefore, it is imperative to (i)
study the robustness of the existing MU methods, (ii) under-
stand the underlying causes of this behaviour and develop
more robust unlearning strategies.

By revealing the weaknesses of existing methods against finetuning and providing a framework to measure concept revival, we aim to guide future research toward developing more secure, reliable, and robust unlearning mechanisms.

raw_sections.abstract:
Abstract
Text-to-image models such as Stable Diffusion, DALL·E,
and Midjourney have gained immense popularity lately.
However, they are trained on vast amounts of data that may
include private, explicit, or copyrighted material used with-
out permission, raising serious legal and ethical concerns.
In light of the recent regulations aimed at protecting in-
dividual data privacy, there has been a surge in Machine
Unlearning methods designed to remove specific concepts
from these models. However, we identify a critical flaw in
these unlearning techniques: unlearned concepts will re-
vive when the models are fine-tuned, even with general or
unrelated prompts. In this paper, for the first time, through
an extensive study, we demonstrate the unstable nature of
existing unlearning methods in text-to-image diffusion mod-
els. We introduce a framework that includes a couple of
measures for analyzing the stability of existing unlearning
methods. Further, the paper offers preliminary insights into
the plausible explanation for the instability of the mapping-
based unlearning methods that can guide future research
toward more robust unlearning techniques. Codes 1 for im-
plementing the proposed framework are provided.
raw_sections.introduction:
1. Introduction
The past decade has witnessed a rapid qualitative growth
in text-to-image models [7, 23, 24, 32, 33], leading to
its widespread attention and adoption. Models such as
DALL·E [2, 30, 31], MidJourney [22], and Stable Dif-
fusion [27, 32] enable high-quality image generation from
simple text prompts, opening applications across art, de-
sign, advertising, gaming, and even medical imaging. With
millions worldwide adopting these tools for academic and
commercial purposes, their transformative potential is ev-
ident, as illustrated by MidJourney’s 16 million active
*Equal contribution.
1https://github.com/NGK2110/TIU
users [22] and the generation of over2 million images daily
by DALL·E and Stable Diffusion [1, 37]. However, this
widespread use raises critical concerns about data privacy,
security, fairness, and intellectual property, as these models
rely on vast datasets that often include sensitive, personal,
or copyrighted content [35].
In response to these privacy concerns, regulations such
as the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) [28] and
the California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) [5] provide
users the Right to be Forgotten [16], promoting the devel-
opment of Machine Unlearning (MU) techniques [3, 13].
MU aims to remove specific data or concepts from a
model without the computational expense of retraining from
scratch. While initially explored in classification mod-
els [4, 13, 14, 36], MU in generative models, particularly
diffusion-based ones, poses unique challenges due to the
models’ complex, distribution-based learning. Ensuring
these models forget sensitive or harmful information while
retaining general functionality remains an open problem.
Machine Unlearning in text-to-image models focuses on
erasing specific concepts from the model’s generative ca-
pacity. The general goal of MU in this context is to ensure
that when a model is conditioned on a prompt related to the
target or “unlearned” concept, it no longer generates im-
ages reflecting that concept. These target concepts can be
Not Safe For Work (NSFW) content [11, 15, 17, 21, 34],
objects [10–12, 15, 19–21, 39, 41] or artistic styles [11, 12,
15, 19–21, 39, 41]. In other words, the unlearned concept is
the one we want the model to “forget”. To achieve this, un-
learning algorithms typically remap the target concept to a
surrogate concept. This means that when the model is con-
ditioned on the unlearned concept, it produces images as-
sociated with the surrogate concept instead. The surrogate
concept can be a general or neutral concept [12, 15, 19], a
random concept [10], or even an empty string [11, 39].
Despite these efforts in Machine Unlearning, we have
identified a critical vulnerability in the current state-of-the-
This CVPR paper is the Open Access version, provided by the Computer Vision Foundation.
Except for this watermark, it is identical to the accepted version;
the final published version of the proceedings is available on IEEE Xplore.
13393

art methods for text-to-image models. Specifically, we
observe that when these unlearned models undergo fine-
tuning, even with general or unrelated concepts, the pre-
viously unlearned concepts can resurface in the generated
images. This unintended revival undermines the unlearning
process’s effectiveness and poses significant risks to data
privacy and legal compliance. The ability of an unlearned
model to regenerate sensitive or copyrighted content after
benign fine-tuning highlights a fundamental weakness in
the existing MU approaches for generative models. Such
vulnerabilities could lead to unauthorized dissemination of
protected content, violating individual privacy rights and in-
tellectual property laws. Therefore, it is imperative to (i)
study the robustness of the existing MU methods, (ii) under-
stand the underlying causes of this behaviour and develop
more robust unlearning strategies.
Contributions of the paper can be summarized as follows.
• Systematic evaluation of MU methods : We conduct a
comprehensive study of the existing MU methods in text-
to-image diffusion models, demonstrating that unlearned
concepts are revived across all examined approaches. Our
analysis includes all the leading methods — SalUn [10],
ESD [11], EDiff [39], CA [19], MACE [20], SPM [21],
SA [15], Receler [17] and UCE [12] —capturing the
full spectrum of current state-of-the-art unlearning tech-
niques. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work
to identify and discuss this issue in depth, examining the
robustness of unlearning. We believe this work brings an
unknown but critical aspect of the MU methods into light,
paving the way to a more resilient framework for unlearn-
ing.
• Concept Revival Framework : We introduce a frame-
work demonstrating that fine-tuning revives the unlearned
concepts. For each target concept, the proposed frame-
work presents a customized curriculum of concepts in the
increasing order of relevance to fine-tune the unlearned
model. During the fine-tuning, the framework enables the
measurement of the revival of the target concept via a cou-
ple of simple measures: classification accuracy and CLIP
score.
• Analysis and insights about the unlearning process :
We study the fragility of the existing MU methods with
respect to two important axes:
– Semantic relevance of the surrogate concept to the tar-
get concept: we analyze how mapping to surrogate
concepts of varying semantic relevance (to the target
concept) affects the stability of the resulting unlearn-
ing.
– Modules affected during the unlearning: we examine
how the nature of model parameters modified during
unlearning, (i.e., cross-attention module vs. the rest),
affects the unlearning.
Further, we hypothesize a plausible explanation for the in-
stability of the mapping-based MU methods - fine-tuning
the unlearned model disrupts the mapping between the
target and surrogate concepts. We provide empirical ev-
idence for this hypothesis over some of the popular MU
methods [11, 12, 19], in terms of the target concept’s un-
learning objective function value during the fine-tuning.
raw_sections.experiments:
4. Experiments - Studying the Vulnerability
We investigate the critical vulnerability aforementioned in
the current Machine Unlearning (MU) methods for text-
to-image diffusion models. Specifically, we demonstrate
through extensive experiments that fine-tuning an unlearned
model can lead to reviving previously unlearned concepts.
raw_sections.conclusion:
6. Conclusions
We present a critical vulnerability in the current Machine
Unlearning techniques for text-to-image diffusion models.
Our study emphasizes the need for (i) more robust tech-
niques that can erase the semantic space of the target con-
cept in the latent space of the model, in which case fine-
tuning can never revive the target concept, or/and (ii) the
method should be able to achieve mapping of target con-
cept to a surrogate concept which is robust to finetuning.
By revealing the weaknesses of existing methods against
finetuning and providing a framework to measure concept
revival, we aim to guide future research toward developing
more secure, reliable, and robust unlearning mechanisms.
Below, We list some interesting and important directions to
further our understanding of the unlearning process.
• Rigorous understanding of why the finetuning process re-
vives the unlearned concepts across several unlearning
approaches. Though challenging, it is of utmost impor-
tance to explain this vulnerability of the existing MU
methods and to achieve robust unlearning.
• Relation between concept revival and finetuning concept.
We have observed that certain finetuning concepts can
cause greater revival while others less. Also, this depen-
dence may vary across unlearned concepts and unlearning
methods. Researching the primary cause of the variance
in dependency between the finetuning concept and the re-
vival of the unlearning concept can aid in assessing the
robustness of the models better.
